text: hey mike, I started scoping out the OAuth2 authorization code flow with PKCE for auth-service. the current implementation skips the code_verifier entirely which is a pretty big gap. gonna draft a design doc today — want to co-own the review?

yeah absolutely, PKCE is critical especially since we're supporting public clients now. I'll loop in @karen_wu for the crypto side — she had opinions on the code_challenge_method (S256 vs plain) last time we discussed identity provider config. also want to make sure the login endpoint redirect flow still works with the new button variants eve is building in design-system-v2

heads up team — kicking off the auth-refactor sprint today. main goals: 1) implement PKCE in the OAuth2 flow 2) add refresh token rotation to prevent token replay attacks 3) migrate session data to the new session_tokens table. @bob_kim can you start on the schema migration for session_tokens? we need columns for token_hash, user_id, issued_at, expires_at, and revoked_at

on it — quick question: for the session_tokens table, should we index on (user_id, expires_at) or (token_hash)? leaning toward a composite index on user_id + expires_at so we can efficiently purge expired sessions, plus a unique index on token_hash for lookups

both — we need the token_hash unique index for the auth middleware to validate tokens on every request, and the composite for the cleanup cron. also add a device_fingerprint column, @karen_wu mentioned we might want device-bound sessions later for the identity provider integration

started refactoring the login endpoint to support the new OAuth2 flow. right now /api/auth/login just does basic credential check + returns a JWT. gonna split it into /api/auth/authorize (starts PKCE flow) and /api/auth/token (exchanges code for access + refresh tokens). any concerns?

looks right @charlie_ross — make sure the /api/auth/token endpoint validates the code_verifier against the stored code_challenge. and we need to set the access token expiry to 15min and refresh token to 7 days per @karen_wu's security guidelines doc

hey eve, wanted to sync on the frontend side of auth-refactor. the login endpoint is getting split into authorize + token exchange endpoints. for the login page, we need to generate a code_verifier on the client side, hash it for the code_challenge, and send it with the initial authorize request. also — are you pulling the button styles from tokens.json yet? I want the login button to use the new primary variant from design-system-v2

yep I saw charlie's message. I'll update the login flow to handle the PKCE challenge — gonna use the Web Crypto API for the code_verifier generation. re: design-system-v2, I'm waiting on @nina_scott's storybook PR to merge before I can pull from tokens.json, but I can hardcode the primary button variant for now and swap it out after

migration for the session_tokens table is ready for review: adds token_hash (varchar 64, unique), user_id (uuid, FK to users), issued_at, expires_at, revoked_at (nullable), device_fingerprint (varchar 255, nullable), and the composite index on (user_id, expires_at). PR is up — #auth-342

nice, reviewing now. one thing — can we add a scope column to session_tokens? we're going to need per-session scopes for the OAuth2 consent flow so the access token only gets the scopes the user approved
good call, I'll add a scopes column as jsonb — that way we can store the array of granted scopes without a join table. pushing the update now

question on the auth middleware — when we validate the access token JWT, should we also check if the corresponding session in session_tokens has been revoked? that adds a DB call per request but it means we can instantly revoke sessions instead of waiting for token expiry

yes, we need revocation checks in the auth middleware. I know the DB hit is a concern — let's add a Redis cache layer. cache the session status with a short TTL (30s) so we get near-instant revocation without hammering the session_tokens table. @karen_wu had a pattern for this in the security playbook, let me dig it up

auth-refactor update: we're splitting the login endpoint into OAuth2-compliant authorize + token endpoints with PKCE. frontend login flow will generate code_verifier client-side. session data moves to a new session_tokens table with proper scopes support. @karen_wu will review the token rotation strategy before we ship to staging. targeting end of week for the backend pieces

thanks for the heads up mike. I'll block time thursday to review the token rotation implementation. main thing I want to verify: when a refresh token is used, the old one must be immediately invalidated in session_tokens — if we detect reuse of an already-rotated refresh token, we need to revoke the entire session family. that's the standard refresh token rotation security pattern

hey bob, for the token exchange endpoint I need to store the authorization code temporarily before it gets exchanged. should I add an auth_codes table or just use Redis with a 10min TTL? the code maps to (code_challenge, redirect_uri, scopes, user_id)

Redis with TTL makes more sense — auth codes are ephemeral by design and we don't need to query them after exchange. less migration churn too. just make sure you delete the key immediately after successful exchange, don't just rely on TTL

starting work on the frontend auth flow changes for auth-refactor. the login page will now do a PKCE authorize → redirect → token exchange flow instead of the current simple POST to /api/auth/login. I'll need to store the code_verifier in sessionStorage between the redirect. @mike_torres does auth-service return the tokens as httpOnly cookies or in the response body?

@eve_johnson access token in response body (stored in memory, never localStorage), refresh token as httpOnly secure cookie with SameSite=Strict. that way the access token lives only in the JS runtime and the refresh token can't be accessed by scripts. @karen_wu approved this pattern in the security review last quarter

makes sense. I'll set up an auth context provider that holds the access token in state and handles silent refresh when the token expiry hits. the refresh will POST to auth-service /api/auth/token with grant_type=refresh_token and the httpOnly cookie goes along automatically

PR #auth-345 is up — implements the /api/auth/authorize endpoint with PKCE support in auth-service. generates the authorization code, stores code_challenge + scopes in Redis, and redirects to the callback URL. @charlie_ross can you review since you're building the token exchange side?

reviewing now. quick thing — I see you're using SHA256 for the code_challenge but the code_challenge_method isn't being stored alongside. we need to persist that so the token endpoint knows how to verify the code_verifier. I'll add a comment on the PR

good catch, fixed. storing code_challenge_method in the Redis payload now. also added validation to reject 'plain' method — we should only support S256 per the RFC. @karen_wu flagged this as a hard requirement

session_tokens migration merged. running it on staging now. also added a background job to purge expired sessions — runs every hour, deletes rows where expires_at < NOW() and revoked_at IS NOT NULL. should keep the table from growing unbounded

nice. one tweak — the purge job should also delete rows where expires_at < NOW() even if revoked_at IS NULL. those are sessions that naturally expired. right now you're only cleaning up explicitly revoked ones
ah you're right, fixing that. should be expires_at < NOW() regardless of revoked_at

hey eve, heads up — the login page error states need to handle new OAuth2 error codes from auth-service (invalid_grant, invalid_client, etc). can you map those to user-friendly messages? also I want the error toast component to use the new semantic colors from design-system-v2 — @iris_wang finalized the error/warning/success palette last week and it's in tokens.json under semantic.feedback
on it. I'll create an error mapping util in the auth module. for the design tokens — is tokens.json published to npm yet or should I import from the monorepo directly?

token exchange endpoint (/api/auth/token) PR is up — #auth-348. handles authorization_code grant type with PKCE verification, issues JWT access token (15min expiry) + refresh token (7 day expiry), stores session in session_tokens table. @alice_chen @karen_wu would love eyes on the token generation logic specifically

reviewing. the JWT payload looks good — sub, iss, exp, iat, scopes. one thing: we should add a jti (JWT ID) claim that maps to the session_tokens row so we can tie the access token back to its session for revocation checks in the auth middleware
smart, adding jti now. I'll use the session_tokens.id as the jti value so the auth middleware can do a single lookup by PK instead of scanning by token_hash

reviewed #auth-348. the PKCE verification looks correct. two concerns: 1) the refresh token should be stored as a SHA256 hash in session_tokens, not plaintext — if the DB is compromised we don't want usable refresh tokens leaking. 2) add a token_family column to session_tokens so we can implement refresh token rotation with family-based revocation

great catches. hashing the refresh token makes total sense — I'll SHA256 it before storing and compare hashes on refresh. for token_family, do we generate a new family UUID on initial login and carry it forward through rotations?

exactly. new family UUID on login. when a refresh token is used, issue a new one in the same family and revoke the old one. if a revoked refresh token in the family is ever reused, revoke ALL tokens in that family — it means the token was stolen. this is the standard token rotation theft detection pattern from the OAuth2 security BCP

saw karen's feedback on token_family — I'll add that column to the session_tokens migration. uuid type, indexed, nullable for backward compat with existing sessions. should have the migration up in an hour
thanks bob, that's perfect

auth middleware refactor PR is up — #auth-351. the middleware now: 1) extracts JWT from Authorization header, 2) verifies signature + expiry, 3) checks jti against session_tokens to see if session is revoked (with Redis cache, 30s TTL), 4) attaches user + scopes to request context. @karen_wu would appreciate a security review when you have a chance
lgtm on the middleware approach. question — how are we handling scope enforcement? does each route define required scopes and the middleware checks the JWT scopes array against it?

yeah exactly — I added a requireScopes() decorator that routes can use. e.g. @requireScopes(['user:read', 'user:write']) on a controller method. the auth middleware populates req.scopes from the JWT and the decorator checks the intersection. clean separation between authn and authz

auth context provider PR is up for the frontend auth-refactor work. handles: storing access token in memory (React state), silent refresh via /api/auth/token with refresh_token grant type, automatic retry of failed requests after refresh, and redirect to login on 401. lmk if anyone wants to review

nice work. does the silent refresh kick in before the access token expiry or after? we should refresh proactively — like when there's 2min left on the 15min token expiry — so the user never sees a 401. also, make sure the loading state on the login button matches the new interaction patterns from design-system-v2. @grace_park added a loading spinner variant to the Button component last week

good call, I'll add a timer that triggers refresh when exp - now < 120s. for the button, I'll switch to the design-system-v2 Button once grace's PR is merged — right now I'm using a local loading state that just disables the button

refresh token rotation is implemented in auth-service. when POST /api/auth/token with grant_type=refresh_token comes in: 1) hash the incoming refresh token, 2) look up in session_tokens, 3) if found and not revoked → issue new access + refresh tokens, revoke old refresh token, same token_family. 4) if found but already revoked → REVOKE ALL tokens in that family (theft detection per @karen_wu's guidance). PR #auth-355

this looks great. one edge case — what happens if the refresh token has expired (past its 7-day window)? we should return an expired_token error and force re-login rather than just generic invalid_grant

good point, adding a specific check for that. will return { error: 'expired_token', error_description: 'refresh token has expired' } so the frontend can distinguish between theft revocation and natural expiry

mid-sprint auth-refactor update: backend OAuth2 flow with PKCE is mostly complete — authorize, token exchange, and refresh token rotation are all in review. auth middleware now validates JWTs against session_tokens with Redis caching. frontend auth context provider is in progress. main risks: identity provider integration hasn't started yet, and we need @karen_wu's final security sign-off on the token rotation theft detection logic before merging to main

bob, quick question on the session_tokens table — what's the estimated row count after 30 days if we assume 10k DAU each with ~3 sessions? want to make sure the purge job + indexes can handle the volume

rough math: 10k users × 3 sessions × 30 days = 900k rows at peak, but the hourly purge job removes expired ones so steady state should be closer to 10k × 3 = 30k active sessions. the composite index on (user_id, expires_at) will handle that easily. I'll add a pg_stat monitor to Datadog just in case

hey charlie, the frontend needs to handle the PKCE flow across redirects. when we call /api/auth/authorize, auth-service redirects to the identity provider and then back to our callback URL with the auth code. where does the identity provider redirect go exactly? is it /api/auth/callback on the backend or a frontend route?

it goes to a frontend route — /auth/callback. the frontend extracts the authorization code from the URL params, then POSTs to /api/auth/token with the code + code_verifier from sessionStorage. that way the code_verifier never leaves the browser
makes sense, thanks

mike, I'm thinking about the identity provider integration. right now auth-service handles everything internally but we need to support external identity providers (Google, Okta) for enterprise SSO. should I build an adapter pattern in auth-service so we can swap between internal auth and external IdP? that way the OAuth2 flow stays the same but the identity verification step is pluggable

yes, adapter pattern is the right call. each identity provider implements a common interface: verifyCredentials(), getUserProfile(), revokeExternalSession(). then auth-service picks the right adapter based on the tenant config. @karen_wu should weigh in on the security implications of delegating identity verification to external IdPs — specifically around token trust boundaries

starting on the identity provider adapter interface for auth-service. will support internal auth + external IdPs (Google, Okta). the authorize endpoint will route to the correct IdP based on tenant config. @charlie_ross this will change how the authorization code is generated — for external IdPs the code comes from them, for internal auth we generate it ourselves. let me know if this impacts the token exchange flow

hmm yeah — for external IdPs the token exchange needs to happen in two steps: first exchange the external IdP's code for their tokens, then issue our own JWT access + refresh tokens stored in session_tokens. PKCE would be between us and the external IdP in that case. I'll refactor the token endpoint to handle both internal and external grant flows

for the IdP adapter — do we need a new table to store IdP configs per tenant? thinking something like identity_providers (tenant_id, provider_type, client_id, client_secret_encrypted, discovery_url, scopes). happy to draft the migration

yeah that's exactly what we need. make sure client_secret is encrypted at rest — @karen_wu will want to verify the encryption approach for storing IdP secrets. use the existing secrets manager integration if we have one, otherwise we need to set one up

auth-refactor frontend update: the login page needs to support both internal login AND external IdP (Google/Okta) flows. @eve_johnson can you add an IdP selector to the login page? the user either enters credentials for internal auth or clicks 'Sign in with Google/Okta' which kicks off the external PKCE flow. I want the IdP buttons to follow the social login patterns from design-system-v2 — @jack_brown had some mockups for this

on it. I'll check with jack for the mockups. the IdP selector will query auth-service for available identity providers per tenant and render the appropriate buttons. the internal flow uses the existing login form, external flows redirect to the IdP's authorize endpoint with our PKCE params

added a new endpoint to auth-service: GET /api/auth/providers?tenant_id=xxx — returns the list of configured identity providers for a tenant so the frontend can render the IdP selector. includes provider_type, display_name, and icon_url for each. no auth required on this endpoint since it's pre-login
lgtm. make sure we're rate limiting that endpoint though — it's unauthenticated and could be used for tenant enumeration if someone brute-forces tenant_ids
good point, adding rate limiting now. 60 requests per minute per IP should be fine

identity_providers table migration PR is up — #auth-362. columns: id (uuid), tenant_id (uuid, FK), provider_type (enum: internal, google, okta, azure_ad), client_id, client_secret_encrypted, discovery_url, allowed_scopes (jsonb), enabled (bool), created_at, updated_at. encrypted client_secret using AES-256-GCM with our KMS key. @karen_wu can you verify the encryption approach is up to your standards?

looking at #auth-362 now. AES-256-GCM is fine for the client_secret encryption. make sure the IV is unique per row and stored alongside the ciphertext. also — we should rotate the KMS key quarterly. I'll add that to the security ops runbook. approved with those changes
will do, thanks for the quick review

alice, QA raised a concern about the login endpoint backward compatibility. we have mobile clients on the old /api/auth/login endpoint that can't do PKCE yet. can we keep the old endpoint alive for a deprecation period? maybe add an X-Auth-Version header so clients can opt into the new OAuth2 flow?

yeah that's reasonable. I'll keep /api/auth/login as a legacy endpoint that still issues JWTs the old way but log a deprecation warning. new clients use /api/auth/authorize + /api/auth/token. we can sunset the old login endpoint after the mobile app ships the PKCE update — @frank_li might know the timeline on that

the /auth/callback page is working — handles the redirect from both internal and external identity providers, extracts the auth code, exchanges it via /api/auth/token with the code_verifier, stores access token in auth context, and redirects to the dashboard. tested with the mock IdP in dev. PR #auth-365 if anyone wants to review

just tested #auth-365 locally — the flow works but the callback page shows a blank screen for ~2s while the token exchange happens. can we add a loading state? I'd love to use the new Skeleton component from design-system-v2 here if it's ready, otherwise a simple spinner is fine
yeah good UX call. I'll add a centered spinner with 'Completing sign-in...' text for now and swap to Skeleton once it's available

identity provider adapter is implemented in auth-service. we now have InternalAuthAdapter, GoogleOAuthAdapter, and OktaOIDCAdapter — all implementing the same interface. the authorize endpoint routes to the correct adapter based on the identity_providers table config. @charlie_ross I updated the token exchange flow to handle external IdP codes — can you check that the two-step exchange still works with your refresh token rotation code?

testing now. initial results look good — the external IdP flow exchanges their code for their tokens, then we mint our own JWT access token + refresh token and store in session_tokens with the token_family. the rotation logic doesn't care about the origin, it just tracks our refresh tokens. one thing — should we store the external IdP's access token somewhere in case we need to make API calls to Google/Okta on behalf of the user?

yes, store it in a separate external_tokens table linked to the session. encrypted at rest same as IdP client secrets. @karen_wu should we treat external IdP tokens with the same rotation policy or just let them expire naturally? they have their own expiry from the identity provider

I'll draft the external_tokens migration. columns: session_id (FK to session_tokens), provider_type, external_access_token_encrypted, external_refresh_token_encrypted, external_token_expiry, scopes_granted. same AES-256-GCM encryption @karen_wu approved for identity_providers
perfect, ship it

auth-refactor is entering the second half of the sprint. backend OAuth2 + PKCE flow is done and reviewed by @karen_wu. identity provider adapters are in for internal, Google, and Okta. frontend login flow supports PKCE + IdP selector. remaining: 1) session management UI (view/revoke active sessions), 2) @karen_wu's final security audit on the full flow, 3) staging integration testing. we're tracking well
for the session management UI — do we want a full page where users can see all their active sessions from session_tokens? like device name, last active, location? and a 'revoke' button per session?

yeah exactly — think GitHub or Google's session management page. show device_fingerprint (parsed into a readable device name), issued_at as 'active since', and a revoke button that calls the session revocation endpoint. use the Table component from design-system-v2 for the layout — @oscar_lee approved the data table patterns last week

I'll add the API for you: GET /api/auth/sessions (returns user's active sessions from session_tokens) and DELETE /api/auth/sessions/:id (revokes a specific session). both require valid access token with 'session:manage' scope
perfect, lmk when those endpoints are up and I'll wire them into the UI

also add a 'revoke all other sessions' endpoint — DELETE /api/auth/sessions with ?except_current=true. users should be able to nuke all sessions except their current one if they suspect compromise. make sure it revokes all tokens in every token_family except the current session's family
on it. good security feature

external_tokens migration is merged. also added a view (v_active_sessions) that joins session_tokens with external_tokens so the sessions API can return everything in one query. the view excludes expired and revoked sessions automatically
nice, that view will simplify charlie's session API a lot. merged!

session management page is in progress. showing active sessions in a table with device info, IP, last active, login method (internal vs Google vs Okta). each row has a 'Revoke' button. there's also a 'Revoke all other sessions' action at the top. using the DataTable component from design-system-v2 which just shipped. will have a PR up by EOD

awesome. one UX thing — when a user revokes a session, show a confirmation dialog first. and after revocation, animate the row out of the table rather than a hard refresh. @lisa_chen has some nice exit animation patterns in the design-system-v2 motion library if you want to use those
love it, I'll add the confirmation dialog + exit animation. makes the revocation feel more intentional

all auth-service backend PRs are merged to the auth-refactor branch: PKCE authorize flow, token exchange with refresh rotation, auth middleware with session validation, identity provider adapters (internal/Google/Okta), session management APIs, and the external_tokens storage. deploying to staging today for integration testing. @karen_wu scheduling the full security audit for next week — does Tuesday work?

Tuesday works. I'll do a full pass on: 1) PKCE implementation correctness, 2) refresh token rotation + theft detection, 3) session_tokens revocation flow, 4) IdP secret storage encryption, 5) auth middleware scope enforcement. will also run a few attack scenarios — CSRF on the callback, token replay, session fixation. expect detailed findings by Wednesday

staging deploy of auth-service is live. tested the full OAuth2 flow end-to-end: authorize → redirect → callback → token exchange → access with JWT → refresh token rotation. all working. the session_tokens table is populating correctly and revocation propagates within the 30s Redis cache TTL. @eve_johnson staging is ready for frontend integration testing

nice! testing now. the login → authorize → callback → token exchange flow works. silent refresh is kicking in correctly before access token expiry. one issue: the IdP selector is showing a 404 for the Okta icon_url — can you check the identity_providers seed data?
ah yeah the icon_url was pointing to a local path that doesn't exist on staging. fixing the seed data to use our CDN URLs. should be up in 5

heads up — noticed the session_tokens table on staging has a few orphaned rows where token_family is NULL. I think those are from the old migration before we added the token_family column. should I write a backfill script to assign families?
yeah go ahead. assign each NULL row its own unique family UUID — they're legacy sessions that don't have rotation chains, so individual families is the safest approach

auth-refactor is on staging and looking solid. @karen_wu's security audit is scheduled for tomorrow. once we get her sign-off, we'll plan the production rollout. the main coordination point is the legacy /api/auth/login endpoint — mobile clients still use it so we need to keep it alive until they ship the PKCE update. shoutout to @alice_chen @bob_kim @charlie_ross @eve_johnson for crushing it this sprint

pre-audit checklist for auth-service: ✓ PKCE with S256 only ✓ refresh token rotation with family-based theft detection ✓ session_tokens with Redis-cached revocation checks ✓ identity provider adapters (internal, Google, Okta) ✓ IdP secrets encrypted AES-256-GCM ✓ scope-based authz in auth middleware ✓ rate limiting on unauthenticated endpoints. @karen_wu this is the scope for tomorrow's review

also worth noting: the token exchange endpoint returns standard OAuth2 error codes now — invalid_grant, invalid_client, invalid_request, expired_token. the frontend maps these to user-friendly messages. that should make karen's attack scenario testing easier since the error responses are predictable

@karen_wu completed the security audit. findings: 1) CRITICAL — the /api/auth/callback endpoint needs to validate the state parameter to prevent CSRF. we're generating state but not checking it on return. 2) MEDIUM — refresh token cookie should have Path=/api/auth/token to limit where the browser sends it. 3) LOW — add Referrer-Policy: no-referrer on auth endpoints to prevent token leakage via Referer header. fixing all three today
on the state parameter fix — I'll add state generation in the authorize endpoint, store it in Redis alongside the code_challenge, and validate it in the callback. classic CSRF prevention for OAuth2

perfect. I'll handle the cookie Path fix and Referrer-Policy header. should be quick — both are one-line changes in auth-service config. once all three are fixed we'll get @karen_wu to verify and we're green for production

session management page PR is merged — #auth-382. shows active sessions from session_tokens, supports per-session revocation with confirmation dialog + exit animation, and 'revoke all other sessions'. also updated the login page to handle the new state parameter in the PKCE flow per @karen_wu's security audit finding

the session management page looks great. just tested on staging — the DataTable component works perfectly here. nice touch with the device icon mapping from the design-system-v2 icon set. the revocation animation is smooth too. lgtm

all three security findings are fixed: state parameter validation in OAuth2 flow, refresh token cookie scoped to /api/auth/token, Referrer-Policy header on all auth endpoints. deployed to staging. @karen_wu can you verify the fixes when you get a chance?

@karen_wu verified all three fixes on staging. the CSRF state parameter check is working — she tested by replaying an old callback URL and got the expected invalid_state error. auth-refactor is cleared for production. 🎉

auth-refactor passed @karen_wu's security audit and is cleared for production! plan: rolling deploy Monday with the feature flag off, then gradual rollout starting at 5% of traffic. legacy /api/auth/login stays active until mobile ships PKCE. huge thanks to the team — @alice_chen @bob_kim @charlie_ross @eve_johnson @karen_wu. this was a clean sprint

running the session_tokens backfill script on production before the rollout — assigning token_family UUIDs to legacy sessions. will take about 10 minutes for ~50k rows. no downtime impact since it's just an UPDATE on nullable columns
backfill complete. all session_tokens rows now have token_family values. production is ready for the auth-refactor rollout

auth-refactor is live in production at 5% rollout behind the feature flag. monitoring auth-service metrics in Datadog — token exchange latency, session_tokens table size, Redis cache hit rate, and error rates on the OAuth2 endpoints. so far looking clean. will bump to 25% tomorrow if no issues

auth-refactor is fully rolled out to 100% as of this morning. zero incidents during the gradual rollout. OAuth2 with PKCE, refresh token rotation, session management, and identity provider support are all live. the legacy login endpoint will stay active for 30 days for mobile client compatibility. great work everyone — @alice_chen @bob_kim @charlie_ross @eve_johnson @karen_wu this was a textbook refactor
